format_detection.evidence: Page 1 is written in continuous prose paragraphs with narration and embedded dialogue in quotation marks (e.g., “and what is the use of a book,” thought Alice). There are no screenplay sluglines (INT./EXT.), no all-caps character cues above dialogue, and no transitions like CUT TO/FADE IN—only section-style headings.
genre_signals: Talking anthropomorphic animal (the White Rabbit speaks and carries a watch/waistcoat-pocket)
Surreal portal/otherworld descent (falling down a rabbit-hole into a deep well and strange hall)
Whimsical, logic-bending internal commentary and impossible objects (e.g., cupboards in the well, “DRINK ME” bottle label)

licensing_friction.evidence: The provided sections do not state the work’s copyright/public-domain status or any adaptation permissions. There is no rights-holder, license grant, or usage restriction language included in the excerpt, so adaptation rights are unclear from the supplied text alone. Because Phase 1 requires using only these sections, this must be treated as likely gatekept/unknown.

latent_choice_architecture.evidence: The text repeatedly presents natural threshold moments with clear options and immediate consequences: Alice chooses to pursue the Rabbit (“burning with curiosity, she ran across the field after it”) and then commits to a point-of-no-return (“down went Alice after it, never once considering how in the world she was to get out again”). The hall sequence is explicitly puzzle-like with multiple doors and tools (“There were doors all round the hall, but they were all locked… a tiny golden key… behind it was a little door… it fitted!”). The midpoint includes an overt navigation fork offered by a character (“In that direction… lives a Hatter… in that direction… lives a March Hare. Visit either you like”).

bounded_agency.evidence: Alice’s core identity is stable across scenes: curious, literal-minded, and socially rule-aware, even while adapting to absurdity. She consistently asserts manners and logic (“Then it wasn’t very civil of you to offer it,” “You should learn not to make personal remarks… it’s very rude,” “It proves nothing of the sort!”). User choices can vary how she expresses that core (polite vs indignant vs defiant) without changing who she is.

emotional_range.evidence: Wonder/curiosity dominates early (“burning with curiosity”), but the excerpt also contains fear/uncertainty (“wondering how she was ever to get out again”), comedy/absurdity (“Do cats eat bats? Do bats eat cats?”; “a grin without a cat!”), social irritation/indignation (“No room! No room!” / “There’s plenty of room!”), and open confrontation/anger in the trial (“Stuff and nonsense!”; “I won’t!”). The closing adds tenderness/nostalgia in the sister’s reflective dream (“simple and loving heart of her childhood… simple sorrows… simple joys”).

recognizability.evidence: The excerpt does not provide any in-text evidence about the IP’s fame, audience awareness, or brand recognition; it is presented only as “SOURCE TEXT UPLOADED.” Since we cannot rely on external cultural knowledge per instructions, recognizability cannot be credited from these sections alone. Therefore it must be treated as unknown/unsupported by the provided text.

replayability_hook.evidence: The world repeatedly behaves like a logic-bending option-space where different routes and interpretations are explicitly validated: “That depends a good deal on where you want to get to,” and “Then it doesn’t matter which way you go.” The setting is filled with modular encounters (locked doors, keys, size-changing implication, directional forks to Hatter vs March Hare) that naturally support multiple paths and replays. Even the ending frames events as a ‘dream’ containing many discrete set-pieces the sister re-experiences in montage, suggesting a revisitable sequence of episodes (“once more the White Rabbit hurried by… once more the rattle of the teacups…”).

ip_audit.editorial_mitigation: Before Phase 2, secure and document adaptation rights in a single source-of-truth brief: confirm public-domain status or obtain a license that explicitly covers interactive adaptation, derivative dialogue, character/setting use, and distribution territories/platforms. If licensing cannot be clarified quickly, develop a parallel ‘Wonderland-adjacent’ original reskin that preserves the excerpt’s functional ingredients (a curiosity-driven protagonist, door/key puzzle hall, a guide offering a directional fork, and an absurd trial) while replacing all protected names/phrases; this keeps production unblocked while legal status is resolved.

entry_point_diagnosis.editorial_diagnosis:
Stakes do not land within the first 60 seconds in the current opening because the text begins in low-energy domestic idleness (heat, boredom, daisy-chain consideration, commentary about pictureless books). A new user can’t yet feel a concrete problem, threat, or point-of-no-return—only mild dissatisfaction.

The first moment where the answer becomes YES is when the White Rabbit demonstrates impossible behavior (talking + pulling out a watch) and Alice physically commits—she springs up and runs. That paragraph contains: (1) an anomaly (a rabbit with a watch), (2) a ticking-clock implication (“I shall be late!”), (3) immediate action (she chases), and (4) a visible objective (catch/see where it goes). This is the earliest true dramatic ignition because it converts mood into motion and implies consequences if she hesitates.

Therefore, everything before that paragraph should be cut. It’s not bad prose; it’s simply passive orientation that delays the user’s first felt stake. The cut preserves Alice’s core trait (curiosity) but demonstrates it through action instead of explanation.

Key world/character elements lost in the cut that must be reintroduced via action/sensory beats in the cold open: the hot, sleepy day; Alice’s boredom/restlessness; the sister/book context (only if needed later—minimize); and that this ‘field/bank/hedge’ is an ordinary place that makes the Rabbit’s watch feel impossible.

format_specific_cut.cut_point: Chapter 1 / Prologue and the Descent — Alice notices the White Rabbit: paragraph beginning “There was nothing so very remarkable in that; … but when the Rabbit actually took a watch out of its waistcoat-pocket…”
format_specific_cut.original_before_cut: 2 paragraphs before cut (the bank-side boredom + daisy-chain consideration).
format_specific_cut.cut_eliminates: Domestic setup, weather/comfort description, internal musing about books and daisies (low-stakes character preface).
format_specific_cut.must_reintroduce: Alice’s baseline normalcy and restlessness (hot day, bored body), and the mundane setting that makes the Rabbit’s watch/anxiety feel like a rupture.

entry_point_diagnosis.cold_open:
Heat shimmers off the grass and the air tastes like dust and crushed clover. You push yourself up from the bank before you’ve even decided to—because something white flashes past your knees.

A Rabbit. Pink-eyed. Close enough that you hear its breath.

It mutters, panicked, “Oh dear! Oh dear! I shall be late!” and—impossible—hooks a paw into a waistcoat-pocket. Metal glints. A watch. It checks the time like it matters.

Your curiosity doesn’t ask permission. You break into a run, skirts snagging at your ankles, heart thumping with the thrill of catching the world doing something it shouldn’t.

The Rabbit darts under the hedge and vanishes into a dark, round hole.

You skid to the edge, peer down into the cool black, and your next step decides whether this is a story you watch… or one you fall into.
entry_point_diagnosis.emotional_promise: The emotional promise of this cold open is: pursuit. A user arrives feeling restless and wanting to chase the impossible before it gets away.

setup.source_moment: Chapter 1 — paragraph beginning: “...but when the Rabbit actually took a watch out of its waistcoat-pocket...” through “...see it pop down a large rabbit-hole under the hedge.” (Alice ignites into pursuit; the hole is the point-of-no-return threshold).
setup.why_it_qualifies: This is the first true dramatic ignition: an impossible anomaly (talking rabbit + watch), a ticking-clock pressure (“I shall be late”), and immediate action with a clear objective (catch/see where it goes). It supports a cold open cut-in and delivers the first BRANCHING choice within 0–3 minutes: commit to the chase/entry vs hesitate.

escalation.source_moment: “In another moment down went Alice after it...” through the hall discovery: “...she found herself in a long, low hall... doors all round the hall, but they were all locked;” then the key/door/garden glimpse: “...the loveliest garden you ever saw... but she could not even get her head through the doorway.”
escalation.why_it_qualifies: Goal becomes visible: get into the garden (a concrete ‘safe/beautiful’ destination). Obstacle becomes clear: Alice’s body/size and the locked architecture (key out of reach; door too small). Stakes compound from ‘chase’ to ‘trapped in a system’ with an achievable-seeming solution (find a way to fit).

breath.source_moment: During the fall and/or early hall absurdity: the “ORANGE MARMALADE” jar (empty) and Alice’s silly ‘Latitude/Longitude’ showing-off; plus the physical comedy of “fancy curtseying as you’re falling through the air!”
breath.why_it_qualifies: Wonder/absurd humor lands as a deliberate release before the more emotionally heavy identity/tears material. It’s a reward beat: the world is playful, not only threatening—inviting the player to enjoy the nonsense (collectible-like jar, wordplay, impossible etiquette).

twist.source_moment: The ‘DRINK ME’ sequence: Alice’s safety-rule reasoning (“see whether it’s marked ‘poison’ or not”) → she drinks → shrinks to ten inches → realizes she forgot the key and cannot reach it → sits and cries.
twist.why_it_qualifies: Moral-weight choice with no correct answer: use an unknown tool to pursue the garden (risk) vs refuse and remain trapped (stagnation). Even ‘wise’ caution doesn’t guarantee safety or success; the consequence is ironic (success at shrinking creates a new failure: key inaccessible). This reframes Wonderland as a place where prudence and impulse both carry costs.

resolution.source_moment: Alice finds the ‘EAT ME’ cake: “either way I’ll get into the garden, and I don’t care which happens!” → she eats → grows huge (“Good-bye, feet!”) → hits the roof and cries → the White Rabbit returns; Alice attempts to ask for help; Rabbit flees; Alice takes the fan/gloves and begins the identity spiral (“Who in the world am I?”).
resolution.why_it_qualifies: Primary arc ‘resolves’ in the sense that Alice has now fully committed to the new rule-set: she will use Wonderland’s labeled tools to force progress (drink/eat), even as it destabilizes her. Seed for next session is planted strongly: the tear-pool, the Rabbit’s dropped items (fan/gloves), and the identity crisis that will drive the social-misstep survival question in Session 2.

beat_map.moment: Cold open on the Rabbit’s impossibility (watch + “I shall be late!”) and it vanishing into the rabbit-hole.
Threshold at the hole: Alice at the edge, deciding whether to commit to the chase into the unknown.
The fall: darkness, cupboards/bookshelves, empty marmalade jar, ‘Latitude/Longitude,’ curtsey joke; landing safe; immediate pursuit resumes into the lamp-lit hall.
Hall problem crystallizes: all doors locked; glass table; tiny key; the curtained small door appears; the key fits.
Reward/wonder: Alice kneels and glimpses the ‘loveliest garden’—bright flowers, cool fountains—so close it hurts.
Back to the table; the bottle appears (‘which certainly was not here before’) labeled DRINK ME; Alice debates poison/rules.
Consequence: shrinking works; then the key is unreachable; slippery table leg; exhaustion; Alice cries—problem deepens.
The cake in the glass box: EAT ME; Alice frames it as a forced gamble (‘either way I’ll get into the garden’).
Growth spiral: ‘Good-bye, feet!’ then roof impact; she grabs the key but can’t fit; renewed crying creates the tear-pool.
Rabbit returns in livery with fan/gloves; Alice chooses how to approach (ask for help vs hide/observe). Rabbit flees; she’s left with the items and the question ‘Who in the world am I?’

next_session_awareness.seed_for_next_session: Plant the tear-pool + Rabbit’s dropped fan/gloves + Alice’s identity wobble. These set up Session 2’s survival-through-consequences loop: she will be physically endangered by her own size changes, socially destabilized, and pulled into unwanted contact (Mouse/pool community; Rabbit authority misread later).

branching_choice_1.source_moment: Chapter 1 — paragraph beginning: “...but when the Rabbit actually took a watch out of its waistcoat-pocket...” through “...see it pop down a large rabbit-hole under the hedge.” (threshold at the hole).
branching_choice_1.narrative_setup: The white rabbit flashes a watch from its waistcoat-pocket and mutters that it will be late, as if time itself is chasing it. It darts under the hedge and vanishes into a rabbit-hole that looks too small to matter and too wrong to ignore. The grass at the edge bends toward the dark like it’s breathing. Your next step decides whether this is a story you watch or a story you enter.
branching_choice_1.choice_question: At the lip of the rabbit-hole, what do you do?

option_a.text: You drop to your knees and go in after the Rabbit without giving yourself time to bargain with fear.
option_a.downstream_effect: *You commit hard to pursuit, and the story will remember you as someone who chooses motion over safety.*

option_b.text: You circle the hedge and study the hole for a safer way down before you risk your body to it.
option_b.downstream_effect: *You enter, but with caution first, and the story will remember you as someone who tries to make the impossible legible before acting.*

option_c.text: You wait at the edge and listen for the Rabbit’s footsteps to confirm what’s real before you follow.
option_c.downstream_effect: *You lose a heartbeat of time and certainty, and the story will remember you as someone who needs proof before committing.*

branching_choice_1.all_paths_arrive_at: Gravity takes you: the rabbit-hole becomes a deep well and you begin to fall past cupboards and shelves.

expressive_choices.source_moment: During the fall: cupboards/bookshelves, the empty “ORANGE MARMALADE” jar, and Alice’s self-talk about Latitude/Longitude.
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: You’re falling slowly enough to think, which is almost worse than falling fast. Cupboards slide by like polite furniture pretending this is normal, and a jar labelled “ORANGE MARMALADE” drifts into your hands—empty, of course. Your thoughts start scrambling for something ordinary to hold onto.
expressive_choices.choice_question: What do you do with the marmalade jar as you fall?

option_a.text: You tuck the jar carefully into a cupboard as if tidiness might keep the world from getting worse.
option_a.downstream_effect: The narration leans into your rule-following composure, marking you as someone who clings to manners even mid-disaster.

option_b.text: You keep the jar with you, because proof matters when reality starts lying.
option_b.downstream_effect: The narration frames you as a collector of evidence, and the jar becomes a small, stubborn token of “this happened.”

option_c.text: You pause and simply watch the shelves slide past, letting the jar go because you need to understand before you manage.
option_c.downstream_effect: The narration slows into observation-first wonder, emphasizing attention over control.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: You continue falling, talking to yourself to fill the dark until you land on a heap of sticks and dry leaves.

expressive_choices.source_moment: Hall discovery: locked doors, glass table, tiny key, curtain, and the small door that the key fits.
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: The hall is long, low, and lit by lamps that make everything look staged. Doors ring the walls—every one locked—until a glass table offers a tiny golden key and, behind a curtain, a little door that seems made for a different kind of person. The key fits with a satisfying click that feels like permission.
expressive_choices.choice_question: With the key in hand, how do you move through the hall?

option_a.text: You test the key on every nearby lock anyway, refusing to trust the first neat answer you’re offered.
option_a.downstream_effect: The narration highlights your skepticism and persistence, turning the hall into a puzzle you intend to solve, not a trap you accept.

option_b.text: You go straight to the curtained door and open it, because the smallest exit is still an exit.
option_b.downstream_effect: The narration sharpens into goal-focus, emphasizing your willingness to commit to the clearest path.

option_c.text: You stand back and watch the hall for a moment, as if it might reveal what it wants from you.
option_c.downstream_effect: The narration tilts eerie and attentive, suggesting the room itself is a character you’re learning to read.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: You kneel and glimpse the bright garden beyond the small passage, close enough to ache and impossible to enter at your current size.

expressive_choices.source_moment: Reward/wonder beat: the ‘loveliest garden’ glimpse—bright flowers, cool fountains—so close it hurts.
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: The garden looks like a promise someone left open on purpose: bright flowers, cool fountains, air that seems easier to breathe. For a moment, the hall’s locked geometry stops being a threat and becomes a frame around something purely beautiful. You’re still stuck, but you’re stuck in front of wonder.
expressive_choices.choice_question: How do you let yourself feel the garden from the wrong side of the door?

option_a.text: You press your face close and memorize every color, as if you can carry the garden with you until you earn it.
option_a.downstream_effect: The narration becomes lush and sensory, rewarding your choice to savor beauty even under pressure.

option_b.text: You whisper a promise to yourself that you will get in, because longing is only useful if it turns into action.
option_b.downstream_effect: The narration turns determined and forward-driving, converting wonder into fuel.

option_c.text: You stay still and simply watch the fountains play, letting the moment be a pause instead of a problem.
option_c.downstream_effect: The narration softens into calm acceptance, granting a deliberate breath before the next gamble.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: You pull back from the door and return to the glass table, where something new waits that was not there before.

branching_choice_2.source_moment: The ‘DRINK ME’ sequence: bottle appears on the table (“which certainly was not here before”), Alice checks for poison, then decides what to do.
values_in_tension: trust in labels/rules versus distrust of appearances
branching_choice_2.narrative_setup: Back at the glass table, a bottle stands where nothing stood before, wearing a neat paper collar that says DRINK ME in beautiful print. You know the stories about children who ignore simple rules and pay for it, and you also know the garden is right there, waiting behind a door your body cannot fit. The bottle is an invitation and a threat, and it is very proud of being both.
branching_choice_2.choice_question: What do you do with the bottle labeled DRINK ME?

option_a.text: You drink from the bottle now, because the only way out of this hall is through the world’s strange tools.
option_a.downstream_effect: *You embrace the gamble, and the story will track you as someone who spends safety to buy momentum.*

option_b.text: You taste only a careful drop and wait to see what happens before you commit to more.
option_b.downstream_effect: *You choose controlled experimentation, and the story will track you as someone who manages risk rather than denying it.*

option_c.text: You set the bottle down untouched and search the hall for another solution that doesn’t ask you to swallow the unknown.
option_c.downstream_effect: *You refuse the gamble for now, and the story will track you as someone who prioritizes self-preservation even if it costs time and opportunity.*

branching_choice_2.moral_weight_confirmation: Each option trades a different kind of harm: bodily danger, lost time, or surrendering control to a world that may punish any choice.
branching_choice_2.talkability_test: Players can argue for hours about whether courage here is drinking, restraint is refusing, or wisdom is controlled testing—because Wonderland punishes all three in different ways.

branching_choice_3.source_moment: White Rabbit returns in livery with fan/gloves; Alice, huge and desperate, chooses how to approach; Rabbit flees and drops items; identity spiral begins.
branching_choice_3.narrative_setup: You are too big for the hall now, wedged under the roof with tears pooling around you, when pattering footsteps return. The Rabbit appears dressed like someone’s servant, muttering about the Duchess, carrying gloves and a fan as if errands are the only law that matters. It is the first person-shaped thing you’ve seen since the fall, and it might be help—or it might be authority that expects you to play a role you don’t understand.
branching_choice_3.choice_question: As the Rabbit comes near, how do you choose to meet it?

option_a.text: You call out politely and ask for help as if you are supposed to be here and the Rabbit is supposed to answer.

option_b.text: You try to speak firmly and stop the Rabbit, demanding it look at you and explain what this place expects.

option_c.text: You stay silent and watch the Rabbit pass, choosing information over contact before you risk being mistaken for someone else.
option_c.next_session_opens: You take the abandoned items after it flees anyway, and the session opens on you weighing whether to use them to blend into Wonderland’s scripts or to keep yourself separate.

path_a.immediate_effect: You fling yourself into the rabbit-hole fast enough to scrape your palms; as you start falling, you snatch at the first passing wall-shelf and end up clutching a labeled jar of ORANGE MARMALADE that is (cruelly) empty.

session_close_design.resolution_prose:
Your voice—small against all that vaulted lamp-lit space—lands and trembles anyway: “If you please, sir—”

For a heartbeat the White Rabbit is close enough that you can see the stitch-work on his livery and the pink shine at the rim of his eye. His whiskers quiver. Then he starts as if you’ve struck him. The fan and the white kid gloves slip from his paws and patter onto the tiles beside your tear-pool with a soft, absurd finality.

He vanishes into the dark passage at a frantic skitter, muttering about the Duchess as though your enormous body is only a draft he can outrun.

Heat presses at your cheeks. You reach down—careful, clumsy—and the fan’s painted ribs click under your fingers. The gloves are cool and too delicate, a servant’s neatness abandoned at your feet. When you lift the fan and draw it through the air, the breeze is real on your wet skin, and with it comes a sharper, stranger thought: if the world expects you to be someone, you can choose how much you’ll play along.
session_close_design.hook_transition:
For one quiet second, the little breeze you make feels like control.

Then the hall answers back: your tears creep outward with a slow, glassy sound, and somewhere in the darkness the Rabbit’s footsteps fade—still fast, still obeying some other clock.

session_end_choice.final_line: Hold the fan a moment longer, feel the thin ribs click, and decide how you want the world to answer you.

entry_point.detail: The session starts at the first true ignition: the Rabbit’s impossible behavior (talking + watch) and Alice’s immediate pursuit. All low-energy preamble is cut, aligning with Law #1 (entry point is a cut).

emotional_promise.detail: The first paragraph promises restless pursuit of the impossible (heat shimmer → white flash → panicked Rabbit with a watch). The emotional contract is clear: chase, wonder, and urgency.

first_choice_timing.detail: Branching Choice 1 (at the rabbit-hole threshold) arrives in the 1:30–3:00 window and is clearly within 300 words of the cold open as written/described.

consequential_choices.detail: Branching Choice 2 (DRINK ME) is not genuinely consequential in its current architecture because the session’s Phase 7 resolution beat still proceeds through the canonical shrink-then-key-unreachable consequence regardless of option, and Path C explicitly says the player delays but then ‘finally does drink (or is forced into change).’ That collapses the branch into a flavor/tempo variation rather than an outcome-bearing divergence. By contrast, Choice 1 and Choice 3 have clear mapped echoes/payoffs across sessions.

stakes_within_60_seconds.detail: Within the opening moments the user gets: an anomaly (talking Rabbit), a ticking-clock cue (‘I shall be late!’), and a vanishing target (rabbit-hole). The implied stake is immediate: act now or lose the impossible.

early_decision_visible_later.detail: Choice 1 has explicit current-session echoes (pace/impulsivity in the fall and hall) and concrete next-session and later-session payoffs (Mouse tone; Rule Forty-two dispute). The session is designed to surface that early stance again later.

breath_beat.detail: A deliberate reward/wonder beat lands at 8:00–10:00 (garden glimpse) and earlier absurdity during the fall supports the release. This satisfies the required breath window.

moral_gray_area.detail: Choice 2 (DRINK ME) is framed as a no-right-answer trade: bodily danger vs lost time vs surrendering control. The moral/values tension is explicit and arguable.

exit_emotional_state.detail: The session ends on the player’s unresolved self-defining decision (how to meet the Rabbit / who to be), with the tear-pool creeping and the Rabbit’s clock-driven flight lingering. The carried-out state is anticipation, not closure or confusion.

talkability.detail: Both the DRINK ME decision and the final ‘how do you meet authority/help?’ decision are immediately discussable. A friend would naturally ask what you did at the hole, whether you drank, and how you handled the Rabbit.

revision_instructions.question: Q4 — Consequential Choices
revision_instructions.phase: Phase 6 (Consequence Maps) + Phase 7 (Session Close / Resolution routing)
revision_instructions.instruction: Make S1_C2 produce a distinct, non-collapsing in-session outcome that changes at least one later beat before S1_C3 (not just narration). Concretely: (1) If Option A (full drink): keep current shrink-fast → key unreachable → tears. (2) If Option B (drop): shrink partially → you can reach the key but still can’t fit the door; this changes the cake beat into a different problem (e.g., you already hold the key when you later grow, altering the Rabbit-return staging and what items you can grab). (3) If Option C (refuse): do NOT force drinking; instead, trigger an alternate escalation cost (time passes, hall ‘responds’—e.g., the Rabbit returns earlier and mistakes you for staff, or the cake appears but the bottle vanishes), so the player arrives at S1_C3 with a materially different state variable (key possession / size band / Rabbit proximity / tear-pool size). Update the beat map and resolution prose routing to reflect these divergent states, and ensure each path has a unique Session 2 opening hook tied to that state.

entry_point_diagnosis.editorial_diagnosis:
The first 60 seconds of the provided pages are dominated by low-stakes comfort and explanation: Alice is bored on a bank, considers making a daisy-chain, and we’re told it’s a hot day and she’s sleepy. Even when the White Rabbit appears, the narration reassures us it’s “not very remarkable” and spends time justifying why Alice isn’t shocked. A new user can’t yet feel urgent stakes—nothing is threatened, no goal is concrete, and the protagonist is still in passive observation mode.

Dramatic energy becomes YES the moment Alice commits to a point-of-no-return: “In another moment down went Alice after it, never once considering how in the world she was to get out again.” Here, the stakes are instantly legible (you might not get out), the motion is irreversible (falling), and the player’s core driver (curiosity overriding caution) is demonstrated through action rather than described. Starting here also aligns with Lorespinner’s retention need: immediate peril + momentum, with the first branching choice naturally arriving as the player decides how to behave while falling (grab/observe/call out), rather than waiting through pastoral setup.

What’s cut: the bank scene, the sister/book complaint, the daisy-chain consideration, and the initial “Rabbit is odd but not that odd” rationalization. These are charming in prose but function as passive runway in an interactive session.

What must be reintroduced through action in the cold open: (1) you are a childlike, rule-aware thinker who talks herself through problems; (2) the inciting anomaly is a dressed White Rabbit with a watch; (3) your motivation is curiosity strong enough to override exit-planning; (4) the setting shift from ordinary outdoors to an impossible rabbit-hole/well.

format_specific_cut.cut_point: Section: “Prologue and the Descent — Alice falls down the well” — opening sentence (“In another moment down went Alice after it…”)
format_specific_cut.original_before_cut: 1 section (~3 paragraphs): the entire “Alice notices the White Rabbit” bank setup up to the rabbit-hole entry
format_specific_cut.cut_eliminates: Pastoral setup, character mood framing (bored/sleepy), explanatory narration that normalizes the weird, and pre-inciting context with no immediate consequence
format_specific_cut.must_reintroduce: Alice was following a White Rabbit with pink eyes, a waistcoat-pocket and a watch; she chose to chase; her curiosity-over-caution trait; the contrast between ordinary day and sudden impossibility

entry_point_diagnosis.cold_open:
Warm air turns cold on your cheeks as you drop—skirts fluttering, hair lifting, the smell of dry earth and old wood rushing up past your nose.

You have just thrown yourself down a rabbit-hole after a White Rabbit in gloves with a watch, and only now does the thought arrive: how, exactly, do you get out again?

The darkness widens into a well. You twist your body to slow the fall, fingers scraping at the wall for anything—anything—to catch. Your nails find smooth plank, then a shelf. Jars. Books. Pegs with pictures swinging as you brush them.

A label flashes by at eye level: ORANGE MARMALADE. You snatch the jar, feel it light—empty—and your stomach drops harder than your body. You can’t throw it; what if someone is below?

You hug it to your chest, hunting for a cupboard to shove it into before the next shelf tears it from your hands.
entry_point_diagnosis.emotional_promise: The emotional promise of this cold open is: vertigo. A user arrives feeling curious and wanting to seize control before the fall decides everything.

setup.source_moment: Cold open at: “In another moment down went Alice after it, never once considering how in the world she was to get out again.” Alice is already falling; she scrapes past shelves, grabs the ORANGE MARMALADE jar, and has to decide what to do with it while still in freefall.
setup.why_it_qualifies: Immediate irreversible jeopardy (can’t easily climb out; uncontrolled fall). The player understands the core driver (curiosity overriding caution) through action. It also naturally supports a first BRANCHING choice within ~300 words: how Alice tries to survive/behave during the fall (grab/slow/observe/call out), which can track curiosity_vs_caution and set tone for later social risk-taking.

escalation.source_moment: Alice lands, sprints after the Rabbit into the lamp-lit hall, finds all doors locked, discovers the tiny golden key, unlocks the 15-inch door, and glimpses the garden she can’t fit into.
escalation.why_it_qualifies: Visible goal becomes concrete: get into the garden (and/or get out of the hall). Clear obstacle: she’s the wrong size; the environment is a puzzle-box of locked exits. Stakes compound from ‘falling’ to ‘trapped with a tantalizing exit.’

breath.source_moment: Absurd, playful internal monologue during size-change: “Good-bye, feet!” plus the imagined shipping address for boots to her own feet (“Alice’s Right Foot, Esq., Hearthrug, near the Fender…”).
breath.why_it_qualifies: This is pure Carrollian comic relief and wonder—language-play and bodily absurdity—landing as a reward beat after the tense puzzle/size mechanics. It also re-centers Alice’s childlike imagination before the next pressure spike.

twist.source_moment: After growing huge and creating the tear-pool, the White Rabbit returns muttering about the Duchess; Alice tries to ask for help (“If you please, sir—”) and the Rabbit panics, drops gloves/fan, and flees. Alice then takes the items and begins the identity spiral: “Who in the world am I? Ah, that’s the great puzzle!” and tests her knowledge (math/geography wrong).
twist.why_it_qualifies: Moral-weight moment with no correct answer: does Alice pursue connection/aid by submitting to the Rabbit’s social frame (polite, small, non-threatening), or assert herself/act independently even if it deepens isolation? Either path costs something: dignity/safety vs agency/alienation. The identity crisis reframes the session’s problem from ‘size’ to ‘self’—a twist in what the player is really managing.

resolution.source_moment: Alice ends this segment holding the Rabbit’s fan and gloves, shrinking again as she fans herself while trying to determine if she’s been ‘changed for Mabel’ and attempting a recitation (“How doth the little—”).
resolution.why_it_qualifies: Primary arc ‘resolves’ in the sense that the initial hall-puzzle attempt has definitively failed and transformed into a new situation: tears + dropped items + identity instability. The seed for next session is planted: the tear-pool and Alice’s compromised self-confidence set up Session 2’s next dramatic question (survive consequences of changes + social missteps) and the upcoming communal absurdity (Mouse/pool/shore). Session-end should be the player’s unresolved decision about how to present herself to the next creature she meets in the pool (reach out vs withdraw; truth vs performance).

beat_map.moment: Cold open: Alice is already falling down the well; shelves/maps/pegs whip past; she snatches the ORANGE MARMALADE jar and must decide what to do with it while still dropping.
beat_map.choice_arrives: YES — Choice 1: how do you handle the fall? (e.g., Claw for shelves to slow down / Focus on spotting the bottom / Call out for the Rabbit / Secure the jar and prioritize not harming anyone below).

beat_map.moment: Landing on sticks/leaves; immediate chase; the Rabbit vanishes; lamp-lit hall with locked doors; Alice tries door after door; discovers glass table + tiny golden key; finds hidden curtain door; key fits.
Alice kneels and glimpses the garden; longing crystallizes the goal; she returns to table and finds bottle labeled DRINK ME; she debates poison rules and decides what to do.
beat_map.choice_arrives: YES — Choice 2: drink now / test cautiously (sip, inspect, delay) / refuse and search for another method. (Tracks risk_tolerance_vs_self_preservation; consequence planned: timing/degree of size change and composure.)

beat_map.moment: Size-change wonder + comedy: telescope shrinking; candle-flame speculation; then later the ‘Good-bye, feet!’ bit and boot-shipping nonsense as her body becomes absurdly unmanageable.
beat_map.choice_arrives: YES — Expressive: what do you do with the absurdity? (Laugh it off / narrate it like a lesson / get angry at your own body).

beat_map.moment: Practical failure: she’s the right size but forgot the key; can’t climb the glass table; cries; finds cake labeled EAT ME and commits to ‘either way I’ll get into the garden.’
After cake: she grows huge, hits the roof, grabs the key, but still can’t fit; collapses into gallons of tears, forming a pool in the hall.
The White Rabbit returns (Duchess pressure); Alice, desperate, tries to ask for help; Rabbit panics and flees, dropping gloves and fan; Alice picks them up and fans herself.
beat_map.choice_arrives: YES — Choice 3 (moral-weight): after being rejected, do you (A) pursue the Rabbit anyway (chase authority), (B) stay and stabilize yourself/your size and prepare to face whoever comes next, or (C) retreat into self-testing/‘Who am I?’ and try to regain identity through rules/recitation. No option is ‘correct’; each trades agency, safety, and social connection differently.

beat_map.moment: Resolution into new status quo: Alice’s identity spiral (Ada/Mabel; wrong math/geography; begins a recitation) while the tear-pool remains as the immediate environment she’ll have to deal with next session.
beat_map.choice_arrives: YES — Expressive end-cap to color next session: how do you frame what’s happening? (I’m failing / The world is cheating / I can adapt if I find the rules).

next_session_awareness.seed_for_next_session: The tear-pool + Alice’s destabilized self-concept + the dropped fan/gloves (a ‘misdelivered errand’ vibe) set up the next encounters in the pool (Mouse/others) where social missteps and survival consequences collide.

branching_choice_1.source_moment: Cold open: Alice is already falling; shelves and cupboards whip past; she has the empty ORANGE MARMALADE jar in hand and must decide what to do with it mid-fall.
branching_choice_1.narrative_setup: You are already in freefall, hair and pinafore floating as the well’s shelves blur past like a library in a storm. The jar marked ORANGE MARMALADE is light and empty in your hand, but it is still a hard thing to drop on whatever may be below. There is no time to plan a landing—only time to choose what kind of girl you are while you fall.
branching_choice_1.choice_question: What do you do with the marmalade jar as the well swallows you?

option_a.text: You wedge the jar into the next cupboard you pass, refusing to let your fall end by hurting someone else.
option_a.downstream_effect: *You stabilize your grip and start scanning the walls for anything useful, anchoring your courage in careful, considerate action.*

option_b.text: You keep the jar clutched to your chest like proof this is real, determined to bring something back from the impossible.
option_b.downstream_effect: *You commit to the experience over safety, and your attention shifts to what else you can grab before the dark ends.*

option_c.text: You hold the jar and simply watch the shelves go by, conserving your movements until you understand what the fall is doing.
option_c.downstream_effect: *You go quiet and observant, letting the well reveal its rules before you spend your fear on action.*

branching_choice_1.all_paths_arrive_at: The fall continues—down, down, down—until the thump of sticks and leaves ends it and the lamp-lit hall waits ahead.

expressive_choices.source_moment: In the lamp-lit hall, Alice tries door after door, finds the glass table and tiny golden key, then discovers the hidden 15-inch door and glimpses the garden beyond.
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: The hall is a ring of locked promises, each door a refusal. When the tiny key finally fits, you kneel and see the garden—bright flowers and cool fountains—so close it hurts. Your body is the only thing that doesn’t belong.
expressive_choices.choice_question: How do you react to the garden you can’t fit into?

option_a.text: You press your face to the gap and let yourself want it, because longing is the only map you have.
option_a.downstream_effect: Your narration leans lyrical and hungry, turning the garden into a personal vow rather than a mere exit.

option_b.text: You pull back and inventory the hall like a puzzle-box, because wanting something doesn’t open doors.
option_b.downstream_effect: Your narration becomes crisp and practical, emphasizing objects, distances, and possible mechanisms over emotion.

option_c.text: You stay kneeling and listen, because if this place has inhabitants, the garden might not be safe at all.
option_c.downstream_effect: Your narration grows wary and sensory, heightening small sounds and the feeling of being watched.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: You return to the glass table, where a bottle labeled DRINK ME waits as if it has been placed for you.

expressive_choices.source_moment: Comic wonder during size-change: shrinking like a telescope, candle-flame speculation, and the 'Good-bye, feet!' boot-shipping nonsense as her body becomes absurd.
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: Your body obeys a rule no one taught you, stretching or shrinking with the casual certainty of a joke. Your feet drift away like they belong to someone else, and your mind scrambles to make manners out of nonsense. The world is unbearable—so you make it speakable.
expressive_choices.choice_question: What do you do with the absurdity of your own changing body?

option_a.text: You laugh at yourself and narrate it like a story you’ll tell later, because humor keeps fear from swallowing you.
option_a.downstream_effect: The tone turns bright and buoyant, making the impossible feel briefly friendly.

option_b.text: You lecture yourself through it, because if you can name rules then you can pretend you’re in control.
option_b.downstream_effect: The tone becomes mock-sensible and schoolroom-sharp, turning wonder into a checklist.

option_c.text: You go quiet and let the strangeness happen, because reacting only makes you feel more ridiculous.
option_c.downstream_effect: The tone becomes hushed and inward, letting the comedy sit beside a thin thread of dread.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: The practical problem returns: the key is out of reach, the table is slippery, and tears come faster than solutions.

expressive_choices.source_moment: After shrinking and forgetting the key, Alice cries, finds the cake labeled EAT ME, and commits to 'either way I’ll get into the garden.'
expressive_choices.narrative_lead_in: You are small enough to fit the door and still too small to reach the key, trapped by your own success. Under the table sits a little glass box with a cake that speaks in currants: EAT ME. The hall offers you another tool with no guarantee it won’t make everything worse.
expressive_choices.choice_question: Before you act, what do you do with the feeling that you’re being tested?

option_a.text: You scold yourself into motion, because tears don’t change locks and you refuse to be helpless.
option_a.downstream_effect: The voice turns stern and self-commanding, framing the next actions as discipline rather than desperation.

option_b.text: You forgive yourself for crying, because anyone would break under this much wrongness.
option_b.downstream_effect: The voice turns tender and human, making the hall feel less like a punishment and more like a trial.

option_c.text: You pause and watch the room, because the safest move is understanding what it wants from you.
option_c.downstream_effect: The voice turns investigative, emphasizing patterns and timing over immediate emotion.

expressive_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: You commit to the cake’s gamble, and the consequences of size come roaring back—too large, too loud, too late.

branching_choice_2.source_moment: The White Rabbit returns muttering about the Duchess; Alice tries to ask for help; the Rabbit panics, drops gloves and fan, and flees into the darkness.
branching_choice_2.narrative_setup: The patter of feet returns, and with it the only other living certainty you’ve seen in this place: the White Rabbit, dressed and hurried and important. You try to speak—polite, desperate—and he recoils as if your voice is an offense, dropping his gloves and fan like he’s shedding blame. He vanishes into the dark, leaving you with his things and your own heat and humiliation.
branching_choice_2.choice_question: How do you respond to the Rabbit’s fear of you?

option_a.text: You call after him in the same polite register, because if you follow the script maybe someone will finally answer you.
option_a.downstream_effect: *You double down on manners and deference, and your next encounters will read you as someone trying to belong—even when it costs you leverage.*

option_b.text: You raise your voice and demand he stop, because being treated like a mistake makes you want to become a problem.
option_b.downstream_effect: *You assert yourself against authority, and your next encounters will carry the heat of confrontation—gaining agency while raising the risk of backlash.*

option_c.text: You let him go and watch where he disappears, because information is safer than pleading with a creature that panics.
option_c.downstream_effect: *You choose restraint and observation, and your next encounters will begin from caution—more prepared, but more isolated.*

branching_choice_2.moral_weight_confirmation: Each option sacrifices something real: politeness risks erasing you, defiance risks punishment, and silence risks loneliness.
branching_choice_2.talkability_test: Players can argue for all three: “Manners are a survival tool,” “You can’t let authority misname you,” or “Chasing approval is how you get trapped.”

branching_choice_3.source_moment: Alice holds the Rabbit’s fan and gloves, begins shrinking again while fanning herself, and spirals into 'Who in the world am I?' as the tear-pool surrounds her.
branching_choice_3.narrative_setup: The fan moves the hot air, and with each flutter your body answers with another shift, smaller and smaller, as if you are being edited mid-sentence. The pool of your own tears waits to receive you, and the hall feels suddenly enormous—too much world for one uncertain name. You can feel an encounter coming, but you can’t decide what face to meet it with.
branching_choice_3.choice_question: As you shrink toward the tear-pool, who do you decide to be when the next creature notices you?

option_a.text: You cling to being Alice and rehearse your proper introduction, because a steady name is the only raft you trust.
option_a.next_session_opens: You hit the water trying to keep composure, then surface determined to speak first—introducing yourself to the nearest swimmer with practiced politeness.

option_b.text: You decide you’ll become whatever this place requires, because survival matters more than consistency.
option_b.next_session_opens: You surface quietly and study the other swimmers, ready to mirror their language and customs before you reveal anything about yourself.

option_c.text: You refuse to choose yet and keep testing your memory, because you won’t perform a self you can’t verify.
option_c.next_session_opens: You surface distracted and half-reciting, and the first creature to approach you catches you mid-proof—forcing you to respond while you still don’t feel real.

path_a.immediate_effect: Alice rams the ORANGE MARMALADE jar into a passing cupboard; the jar catches with a sharp ceramic clink and her fingers scrape on a shelf-bracket, leaving her palm stinging as she keeps falling empty-handed.
path_a.current_session_echo: Because she let the jar go, she later hesitates before using the White Rabbit’s fan; she tests one cautious flutter first, narrating to herself that she’s “not going to do anything rash,” which slows the shrinking beat and makes the hall feel even more watchful.
path_a.next_session_payoff: Session 3 opening at the Rabbit’s house: when the Rabbit’s servants shove the pebble-stones through the window (“It was all made of little cakes”), Alice refuses to eat the first one immediately; she holds it up, sniffs it, and says, “I won’t be the cause of another accident,” delaying the growth by a beat and changing the timing of the first arm-in-the-window gag.
path_a.later_session_legacy: Session 5, Queen’s procession: when the Queen barks “Off with her head!” at a minor offender, Alice’s internal line explicitly recalls the jar choice—“I wouldn’t even drop a jar on a stranger”—and she steps half a pace forward before catching herself, establishing her as someone whose caution is ethical, not fearful.

path_b.immediate_effect: Alice keeps the jar clutched to her chest; when she hits the heap of sticks and leaves, the jar thumps against her ribs and survives, and she immediately checks it like a trophy—proof she can carry something out of the impossible.
path_b.current_session_echo: She uses the jar as a comfort-object and measuring tool: when the Rabbit drops the gloves and fan, she compares their size to the jar’s mouth, turning the scene into a tactile inventory (jar, gloves, fan) that pushes her toward grabbing and using Wonderland objects faster—even when it’s risky.
path_b.next_session_payoff: Early Session 3 (post-pool, pre-house chaos): on the shore, during the ‘Caucus-race’ aftermath, Alice produces the marmalade jar to ‘offer’ something useful; the Lory peers into it and deadpans, “It’s empty,” turning Alice’s souvenir into a public joke that still validates the player’s impulse to bring proof.
path_b.later_session_legacy: Session 7 trial: when evidence is presented as nonsense (the paper of verses), Alice’s thought explicitly links back—“I kept an empty jar as evidence; Wonderland keeps empty words”—and she is quicker to call out the court’s ‘proof’ as performative.

path_c.immediate_effect: Alice keeps the jar but goes limp and observant; she rotates slightly in the fall and notices one shelf labeled ‘MAPS’ and another labeled ‘MARMALADE’ flashing past—two labels that don’t match their contents—before the leaves break her landing.
path_c.current_session_echo: Her restraint makes her more pattern-driven: after the Rabbit flees, she doesn’t immediately chase or plead; she studies the corridor’s acoustics and the direction of his footfalls, and when she fans herself, she watches the fan’s effect like an experiment rather than a panic-response.
path_c.next_session_payoff: Session 3, inside the Rabbit’s house sequence: when she grows too large and her arm wedges in the window, she remembers the ‘labels don’t match’ observation and says, aloud, “Names aren’t instructions,” right before refusing the first shouted suggestion from outside—changing the dialogue cadence and giving the player a distinct ‘rule-reading’ posture in the siege.

path_a.immediate_effect: Alice calls after the Rabbit with exaggerated politeness—“Sir! If you please!”—and the darkness answers with only his retreating mutter; the dropped gloves and fan remain on the floor like a failed audition for belonging.
path_a.current_session_echo: Her deference carries into the tear-pool beat: when she later addresses the Mouse, she opens with a formal, careful greeting (“O Mouse, do you know the way out of this pool?”), which makes the Mouse’s offense at her talk of Dinah land harder—her politeness becomes the very thing that misfires.
path_a.next_session_payoff: Session 5, Queen arrival and questioning: when the Queen fixes Alice with “Who are you?” Alice reflexively answers in the same ‘proper introduction’ register—name first, then courtesy—giving the Queen an opening to steamroll her with etiquette-as-weapon before Alice can recover.
path_a.later_session_legacy: Session 7 courtroom: during the Rule Forty-two dispute, Alice initially tries to argue ‘within procedure’ (“If there’s a rule, it ought to be written down properly”), which delays her open rupture by one exchange and makes the eventual break feel like a snapped restraint.

path_b.immediate_effect: Alice shouts after the Rabbit—“Stop!”—and her voice cracks off the corridor walls; in response, the Rabbit’s pace audibly accelerates, and a door slams somewhere ahead, cutting off pursuit and leaving her holding his gloves and fan like confiscated property.
path_b.current_session_echo: That heat bleeds forward: when she begins shrinking again, she fans herself with irritation rather than comfort, and her internal monologue shifts from ‘What will become of me?’ to ‘I won’t be treated as a mistake,’ making the tear-pool feel less like pure panic and more like anger at being handled.
path_b.next_session_payoff: Session 5, Queen arrival: when the Queen snaps at someone (“Off with his head!”), Alice’s earlier ‘become a problem’ stance pays off as a specific beat—she blurts, “You can’t just—” and is physically hushed by a courtier, creating a tangible backlash consequence (public silencing) that the compliant path doesn’t trigger.
path_b.later_session_legacy: Session 7 trial: when the King reads the nonsensical verses, Alice interrupts earlier and more directly (“That proves nothing!”) and the court reacts with immediate hostility—cards rustle, jurors crane—setting up a more aggressive escalation into the final eruption.

path_c.immediate_effect: Alice lets the Rabbit go and tracks his exit; she kneels, touches the dust where his feet skidded, and notes the exact doorway he vanished through—left-hand arch, third shadow—before picking up the gloves and fan.
path_c.current_session_echo: Her information-first posture changes the pool/shore social rhythm: she speaks less, watches more, and when the Mouse begins the ‘dry’ plan, she’s already scanning the bank for the largest rock and the nearest ‘ring’ space—making her feel prepared but socially distant.
path_c.next_session_payoff: Session 5, Queen questioning: Alice recognizes the same ‘left-hand arch / third shadow’ doorway motif in the palace approach (a repeated architectural rhyme) and realizes she’s being herded; she chooses a specific physical move—stepping to the side of the procession line to see who blocks her—gaining a moment of tactical clarity that the other paths don’t get.

path_b.immediate_effect: N/A — session ends on this choice.

path_c.immediate_effect: N/A — session ends on this choice.
path_c.next_session_opening: Alice surfaces mid-recitation—trying to prove herself by memory (“How doth the little—”)—and the first creature to approach (the Mouse) catches her in the broken verse; she has to respond while still sounding unreal, half-poem, half-girl.
path_c.next_session_payoff: Session 3, Caterpillar identity argument: when pressed, Alice demands verification (“What do you mean, who am I? How would I tell?”) and the Caterpillar’s slow, needling questions become a direct payoff to her refusal to perform an unverified self—making the scene feel like an interrogation she invited.
path_c.later_session_legacy: Session 7 trial: when ‘evidence’ appears as a paper of verses, Alice is uniquely primed to attack the logic of proof-by-rhyme; she recognizes the trap because she has personally tried (and failed) to use verse as identity-proof, sharpening her dismantling of the court’s nonsense.

session_close_design.resolution_prose:
The fan’s paper ribs tick against your fingers—tick, tick—and the hot air you push away seems to take pieces of you with it. Your sleeves loosen. Your shoes go suddenly heavy, as if they belong to someone taller. The hall stretches upward into lamp-lit distance, and the pool of your own tears—salt, warm, and absurdly deep for something you made by crying—waits at your feet like a small, private sea.

You try the sums again, the capitals again, and each answer slips sideways. Names you know—Ada, Mabel—float up like labels on jars, none of them fitting the mouth of you. The fan keeps fluttering. You keep shrinking. The gloves hang from your wrist like borrowed manners.

At last your toes meet the tear-water. Cold climbs your ankles. You don’t stop; you can’t. The surface takes you with a soft, swallowing sound, and the world becomes ripples and lamplight broken into a thousand bright coins.
session_close_design.hook_transition: For one breath, you simply bob there, listening to your own small splashes and the distant drip of the hall. Then something else moves in the water nearby—steady, purposeful strokes—and a whiskered face turns toward you as if it has been expecting you to appear.

first_choice_timing.detail: Branching Choice 1 (marmalade jar) is embedded in the cold open and clearly arrives within the first ~300 words of the experience.

As written, the session either (a) has 4 branching choices (violating the 3-choice architecture) or (b) has an un-mapped branching choice (DRINK ME) with no verified downstream plan.
